county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Adams County formed 1799 as one of Mississippi Territory's two original counties (with Pickering, later Jefferson). County seat is Natchez, the oldest European settlement on the Mississippi River (French Fort Rosalie, 1716). Natchez served as territorial capital 1798-1802. French colonial (1716-1763), British (1763-1779), Spanish (1779-1798), and American (1798-present) records reflect the complex colonial history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Adams County is one of Mississippi's original two counties (1799) and the seat of Natchez, the oldest European settlement on the Mississippi River (French 1716, British 1763, Spanish 1779, American 1798). The Mississippi Department of Archives and History (MDAH) in Jackson holds microfilm of Adams County deed books, chancery court records, and marriage records from the territorial period. The Chancery Clerk's office in Natchez retains original land records from 1799 and probate records processed through the chancery court system unique to Mississippi. Natchez was a major slaveholding center -- the Historic Natchez Foundation and MDAH hold plantation records, slave schedules, and manumission documents. French and Spanish colonial records for the Natchez District are at MDAH and the Mississippi Provincial Archives (copies from French and Spanish archives). The Natchez Trace collection at MDAH documents early migration. FamilySearch has microfilmed Adams County chancery, circuit, and land records extensively. Church records from Trinity Episcopal (1822), First Presbyterian (1817), and Catholic churches in Natchez provide baptism, marriage, and burial data predating civil registration.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Adams County deed books from 1799; Chancery Clerk in Natchez maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Alcorn County formed 1870 from Tippah and Tishomingo counties. County seat is Corinth, a strategically important railroad junction during the Civil War. Named for Governor James Lusk Alcorn. The county lies in the northeast Mississippi hill country on former Chickasaw Cession lands.
county_metadata.research_tips: Alcorn County was formed in 1870 from Tippah and Tishomingo counties, with Corinth as county seat. Corinth was a strategic railroad junction during the Civil War -- the Battle of Corinth (1862) and Siege of Corinth left extensive military records at NARA. The Chancery Clerk in Corinth holds deed books from 1870 and chancery court (probate) records. For pre-1870 records, check Tippah County (Ripley) and Tishomingo County (Iuka). MDAH has microfilm of Alcorn County land and chancery records. The Northeast Mississippi Museum in Corinth holds local history collections. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Corinth sub-district are at NARA (M1907) and document formerly enslaved families in the area. BLM GLO land patents from the Chickasaw Cession are searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations organized after the war are important sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Alcorn County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in Corinth maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books and court minutes
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Amite County formed 1809 from Wilkinson County. County seat is Liberty. Burned county: 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early deed books and court minutes. Named for the Amite River. Located in southwest Mississippi near the Louisiana border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Amite County (formed 1809 from Wilkinson) suffered an 1882 courthouse fire that destroyed many early deed books and court minutes, making it a burned county. The Chancery Clerk in Liberty holds surviving and reconstructed records. MDAH has microfilm of available Amite County records. For the burned period, researchers should consult BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), Wilkinson County records for pre-1809 entries, and private deed copies filed after the fire for reconstruction. Amite County had a large enslaved population -- plantation records, estate inventories, and slave schedules at MDAH are critical. Freedmen's Bureau field office records for southwest Mississippi (NARA M1907) document post-war African American families. The Amite County Genealogical Society maintains local indexes. Baptist and Methodist church records from the early 1800s are held by local congregations and MDAH.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Amite County deed books from 1809; Chancery Clerk in Liberty maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1882 courthouse fire destroyed many early records including deed books and court minutes; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Attala County formed 1829 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820. County seat is Kosciusko, named for the Polish patriot Tadeusz Kosciuszko. Located in central Mississippi, the county borders Choctaw reservation lands. The Natchez Trace passes through the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Attala County (formed 1829 from the Choctaw Cession of 1820) has its seat in Kosciusko, named for the Polish hero. The Chancery Clerk in Kosciusko holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court probate records. MDAH has microfilmed Attala County land records, marriage books, and chancery minutes. Attala County is within the Choctaw Cession lands -- BLM GLO land patents at glorecords.blm.gov document original federal land entries. The county borders the Mississippi Band of Choctaw Indians reservation area, and Choctaw-related records are at NARA (Record Group 75) and the Choctaw Agency. Tax rolls from the 1830s at MDAH list early settlers and property. FamilySearch has digitized Attala County chancery and circuit clerk records. The Natchez Trace Parkway passes through the county, and early migration records connect settlers from Tennessee, the Carolinas, and Georgia.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Attala County deed books from 1829; Chancery Clerk in Kosciusko maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Benton County formed 1870 from Marshall and Tippah counties. County seat is Ashland. Located in north-central Mississippi on former Chickasaw Cession lands. One of the smaller and more rural Mississippi counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Benton County (formed 1870 from Marshall and Tippah) has its seat in Ashland. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1870. For pre-1870 records, researchers must check Marshall County (Holly Springs) and Tippah County (Ripley). MDAH has microfilm of Benton County records. BLM GLO land patents from the Chickasaw Cession (glorecords.blm.gov) document original land entries in the area. Benton County is small and rural -- church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations are particularly important for vital event documentation. Census records from 1870 forward capture the county's population. Tax rolls at MDAH help trace landholding families. The county had a significant African American population, and Freedmen's Bureau records for the Holly Springs sub-district cover the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Benton County deed books from 1870; Chancery Clerk in Ashland maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Bolivar County formed 1836 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Cleveland (previously Bolivar and Rosedale). A quintessential Mississippi Delta county along the Mississippi River with vast cotton plantations. Delta State University is in Cleveland.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bolivar County (formed 1836 from Choctaw Cession) is a Mississippi Delta county with Cleveland as county seat. The Chancery Clerk in Cleveland holds deed books from 1836 and chancery court records. Bolivar County was one of the wealthiest cotton-producing counties in America before the Civil War, with an enslaved population that vastly outnumbered whites. MDAH holds extensive plantation records, slave schedules, and estate inventories for Bolivar County. Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA (M1907) are essential for tracing African American families in the Delta. The Delta blues heritage is documented at Delta State University archives in Cleveland. BLM GLO land patents document Choctaw Cession entries. Levee board records at MDAH document families involved in Mississippi River flood control. The 1927 Great Flood devastated the Delta -- Red Cross relief records at NARA document displaced families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Bolivar County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Cleveland maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Calhoun County formed 1852 from Chickasaw Cession lands, Lafayette, and Yalobusha counties. County seat is Pittsboro. Located in north-central Mississippi hill country. Named for Vice President John C. Calhoun.
county_metadata.research_tips: Calhoun County (formed 1852 from Chickasaw Cession lands, Lafayette, and Yalobusha) has Pittsboro as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court probate records from 1852. For pre-1852 land entries, check BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) from the Chickasaw Cession. MDAH has microfilm of Calhoun County records. Parent county records for Lafayette (Oxford) and Yalobusha (Water Valley) may contain pre-formation entries. Calhoun County is rural and agricultural, and church records from Baptist congregations are important supplements to civil records. FamilySearch has digitized Calhoun County chancery clerk records. Tax rolls and personal property assessments at MDAH list enslaved persons by owner before the Civil War.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Calhoun County deed books from 1852; Chancery Clerk in Pittsboro maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Carroll County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830. County seat is Carrollton, with two judicial districts (Carrollton and Vaiden). Located at the transition between the Delta and the loess hills. Named for Charles Carroll of Carrollton, last surviving signer of the Declaration of Independence.
county_metadata.research_tips: Carroll County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) has Carrollton as county seat and maintains two judicial districts (Carrollton and Vaiden). The Chancery Clerk in Carrollton holds deed books from 1834 and chancery court records. Carroll County was settled rapidly after the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830) opened Choctaw lands. MDAH has microfilmed Carroll County records extensively. BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document original federal land sales in the Choctaw Cession. The county had a significant enslaved population on cotton plantations. Estate inventories and slave schedules at MDAH are important for African American research. Freedmen's Bureau records for the Grenada sub-district cover Carroll County. The Merrill/Zion Hill plantation records at MDAH document enslaved communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Carroll County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Carrollton maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Chickasaw County formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832 (Treaty of Pontotoc). County seat is Houston. Located in northeast Mississippi. Named for the Chickasaw Nation whose homeland encompassed this area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chickasaw County (formed 1836 from the Chickasaw Cession of 1832) has Houston as county seat. The Chancery Clerk holds deed books and chancery court records from 1836. The county is named for the Chickasaw Nation, whose homeland was in northeast Mississippi before removal via the Treaty of Pontotoc (1832). BLM GLO land patents (glorecords.blm.gov) document the federal land sales that followed Chickasaw removal. MDAH has microfilm of Chickasaw County land records and chancery minutes. Chickasaw Nation removal records at NARA (Record Group 75) document tribal members who remained or left. Church records from Baptist and Methodist congregations organized in the late 1830s are held locally and at MDAH. FamilySearch has digitized available Chickasaw County clerk records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Chickasaw County deed books from 1836; Chancery Clerk in Houston maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.record_losses: 1881 courthouse fire destroyed most early records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Choctaw County formed 1833 from the Choctaw Cession of 1830 (Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek). County seat is Ackerman. Burned county: 1881 courthouse fire destroyed most early records. Named for the Choctaw Nation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Choctaw County (formed 1833 from Choctaw Cession) is a burned county due to the 1881 courthouse fire that destroyed most early records. County seat is Ackerman. The Chancery Clerk holds reconstructed and post-fire records. For the burned period (1833-1881), researchers should use BLM GLO federal land patents (glorecords.blm.gov), MDAH microfilm of any surviving fragments, and private deed copies filed after the fire. Choctaw County was carved from lands ceded by the Choctaw Nation under the Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830). NARA Record Group 75 holds Choctaw removal and allotment records. Tax rolls at MDAH may partially fill gaps. Freedmen's Bureau records for the area document post-war African American families. Census records from 1840 forward are available through NARA and FamilySearch.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: MDAH microfilm of Choctaw County deed books from 1833; Chancery Clerk in Ackerman maintains originals; BLM GLO federal land patents at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Choctaw County Deed Books -- Chancery Clerk / MDAH

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books for Choctaw County from 1833; MDAH holds microfilm copies; Chancery Clerk in True maintains originals; Mississippi is a public land state with BLM GLO patents available online
source_profiles.survival_notes: Significant losses from 1881 courthouse fire destroyed most early records; reconstructed records and surviving fragments at Chancery Clerk and MDAH
